pecially that will enable the college
to return a larger service per dollar,
for the money it spends?
"(c) An estimate of the benefit
farmers have received, for instance,
from investigations and curative rem­
edies provided by the biological de­
partment for such plant diseases as
smut in wheat, potato scab, soil fun­
gi, etc., together with animal diseases
and investigations of same, made by
the division of vetorinary science.
Investigations by Ladd.
"Investigations made by Professor
Ladd to determine the milling value
of wheats which alone closed a spread
of approximately twenty cents per bu­
shel between Durum and fife, and
which now frequently sends Durum
wheat to a premium, and which also
raised the price of velvet chaff sev­
eral cents per bushel. These investi­
gations alone have been worth to
our farmers not less than $3,0'00,0M)
annually. The elimination of poison­
ed and impure food and drugs, false
weights and measures, bogus paints,
etc., from the state, by the division of
chemistry also is worthy of note.
"I mention the above for the reason
that many good men are impressed
with the belief that these services
alone, every single year, are worth
more to the people of the state than
this institution has cost since its or­
ganization. Moreover, the division of
agriculture, by demonstrating the im­
portance of crop rotation, dairying,
by introducing improved varieties of
grasses and cereals, especially corn
and alfalfa, by demonstrating the
value of manure, the necessity for the
siio, conservation of moisture, de­
struction of weeds, etc., have been
worth a few million dollars annually
to the state at large. •.
Exerted Influence.
"(d) As an educational institution.
Has the influence which the agricul­
tural college has exerted upon the
thousands of young men and women
in giving them the best of training
for the farm and the home, and as
far as possible a desire to devote their
energies, either to rural life or as
teachers of agriculture or home econ­
omics in the public schools, as agri­
cultural journalists, institste conduc­
tors, county agents or attaches of
state experiment stations or of the
federal department of agriculture, etc.
been worth the money used for main­
taining the college, and especially
whether the money thus expended has
returned to the state, dollar for dol­
lar, as good results on the average as
dollars spent for other purposes?
"(e) Admitting all or any portion
of the above, the question is a fair
one and worthy the discriminating
analysis of any citizen, as for instance
how can the work of the agricultural
college be improved, or how can each
dollar be made to render better ser­
vice?
Exclusive Industry.
"Since agriculture is the almost ex­
clusive industry of the state and since
farmers pay seven-ninths of the state
taxes, it is inconceivable that any one
would begrudge the agricultural inter­
ests of the state tbo necessary means
for the proper and adequate support
of their agricultural college and ex­
periment station. To improve it,
however, to make it more efficient
and to make each dollar expended for
its support render more efficient ser­
vice is proper and in order. For one
I welcome it. I would also welcome
opinions from farmers as well as
from editors, since they bear the chief
burden of all the state institutions.
"Such questions as the following,
therefore, may well be considered:
"1. 'What is the agricultural col­
lege undertaking that the state as a
whole does not wish it to do?
"All 'hings considered, is the agri­
cultural college spending too much or
too little state money, at present,
about $65,000 annually? The govern­
ment inspectors seem well satisfied
with the purpose for which the fed­
eral funds are expended.
"3. What is the agricultural col­
lege failing to undertake that the tax
payers would have it do?
Urges Constitutional Convention.
"4. Would it not be a good plan,
after all, to call a constitutional con­
vention and reorganize the education­
al sy3tam of the state concentrating
rather than distributing the work?
also reduce the .Legislative assembly
to half the present membership and
reorganize the duties of state officers,
reducing the number to the actual
needs of the state, and if thought

1
FOR THE BELGIANS
Mme. Lalla Vandervelde.
Mme. Lalla Vandervelde, wife of
the minister d'etat of Belgium, is
touring the United States pleading
the cause of the starving Belgians
and collecting funds for their relief.
These funds are spent as far as pos­
sible in the communities in which
they are collected. She already has
received more than a quarter of a
million dollars.
best, establish a commission form of

Fundamental Error.
Tolerating fundamental errors-^-and
there are many such—only postpones
the day of adjustment which must
surely come. The public institutions
of the state were peculiarly -created
and located, but the influence that in­
cubated the present scheme being
now dead, or supposed to be, our past
experience should enable another con­
stitutional convention to act more
wisely.
A board of control for ail the edu­
cational institutions, even if better
than the present system, and the his­
tory of every state creating one re­
pudiates it, is not sufficient. 'Scold­
ing the schools only draws attention
from bigger leaks^fietter get'down
to fundamentals and -build from the
very foundation. The legislative as­
sembly itself is not a paragon of
economy.
Respectfully,
J. H.. WORST.
LURK IN
EVIL SPIRITS THAT
TREES.
It often happens, says a writer in
the January Wide World Magazine,
that a gang of Malay or Javanese
coolies, while engaged in clearing vir­
gin jungle on a rubber plantation,
will leave untouched a tree or group
of trees, rather llian risk the anger
of the penungu or spirit by which
they believe them to be inhabited.
In this case resort is made to the
Tamils natives of India, usually en­
gaged as "tappers" of the rubber
trees, who, though also superstitious,
are more easily tomptsd by a mone­
tary reward.
The following case is absolutely
authenticated 1 an Englishman who
is employed as assistant manager ot
an estate in .Malacca. While a cer­
tain portion of the estate was being
cleared it was noticed that a group
of several trees was left standing in
one spot. The assistant ordered the
Malays to cut them down, but they
refused, saying that these trees were
the abode of an evil spirit.
A day or two later some Tamils
were induced to pile dry branches
round one of the trees, pour kero­
sene over the pile, and set fire to the
tree. While this was 'being done a
log fell on one of the Tamils, cutting
his shoulder to the bone. When the
fire was lighted a snake bit the Tam­
il, who thrust the match in, and, in
spite of the kerosene, the tree refus­
ed to burn.
'Next imorning the assistant ordered
the Tamils to chop the tree down,
which they did, but when the tree
gave way it. fell on one of themen and
nearly killed him.
The third morning the Tamils were
absent from work and on inquiry the
assistant was informed that they
were ill. Supposing them to be mere­
ly malingering, the European asked
the estate doctor to
vgo
round to the
coolie lines with him and examine
the men. Here the doctor found the
whole seven of them' really ill, quite
unable to rise from their beds, and or­
dered their removal to the estate hos­
pital. After this no Tamil, Malay,
Javanese or. Chinese coolie could be
induced to touch these trees.
The manager does not say much
about the coincidence, but so far he
has let the trees stand and if he
should ever want them cut he will
probably have to the work himself,
for everybody inMalacca has heard
the story.

The distinction upon which we must

So much for tho general subject of
Industrialism. Now, Just a word In

the growing growth of diversification
of crops In agriculture. Nevertheless
U will always be true that lu certain
of the southern states cotton will be
the basis of the wealth, the mainstay
of prosperity in the future a In the
past. The cotton crop Is of emrr.iou
consequence to the entire country. It
was the cotton crop of the south that
brought four hundred million dolla"
of foreign gold Into the United States
last year, turning the balance of tra l-
In our favor. The soil and climate of
the south are such that she enjoys n
practical monopoly In the production
of raw cotton. No other clothing ma
terial can be accepted as a substitute
for cotton. I welcome the action of
tho planters in forming a cotton as
sociation, and every assistance sha'.l
bvf given them that can be given them
by the national government. More
over, we must not forget that the
work of the manufacturers in the
south supplements the work of the
planter. It is an advantage to man
ufacture the raw material here and
sell to the world the finished goods.
tinder proper methods of distribution
It may well be doubted whether there
can be such a thing as overproduction
of cotton. LaBt year's crop was near
ly fourteen million bales, and yet the
price was sufficiently high to give a
handsome profit to the planter. The
consumption of cotton increases each
year, and new uses are found for It.
This leads me to a matter of our
foreign relations, which directly con
cerns the cotton planter. At present
our market for cotton Is largely In
China. The boycott of our gooda In
China during the past year was espe
cially injurious to the cotton manu
facturers. This government Is doing,
and will continue to do, all It can to
put a stop to the boycott. But there
is one measure to be taken toward this
end In whlen I shall need the assist
ance of, the congress. We must In
sist firmly on our rights; and China
must beware of persisting in a course
of conduct to which we can not hon
orably submit. But we In our turn
must recognize our duties exactly as
we insist upon our rights. We can
not go into the international court of
equity unless we go in with clean
hands. We can not expect China to
do us justice unless we do China jus
tice. 'The chief cause in bringing
about the boycott of our goods in
China was undoubtedly our attitude
toward the Chinese Who come to this
country. This attitude of ours does
not justify the action of the Chinese
in the boycott, and especially some of
the forms which that action has taken.
But the fact remains that in the past
we have come short of our duty to
ward the people of China, It is our
clear duty, in the interest of our own
wage-workers, to forbid all Chinese of
the coolie class that is. laborers,
skilled or unskilled from coming
here. The greatest of all duties Is
national self-preservation, and the
most important step In, national self
preservation is to preserve in every
way the well-being of the wage-worker.
I am convinced that the well
being of our wage-workers demands
the exclusion of the Chinese coolies,
and it is therefore our duty to exclude
them, Just as It would be the duty of
China to exclude American laboring
men if they became In any way a
menace to China by entering Into her
country, The right is reciprocal, and
in our last treaty with China It was
explicitly recognized an inhering in
both nations. But we should not only
operate the law with as little harsh
ness as possible, but we should Bhow
every courtesy and consideration and
every encouragement to all Chinese
who are not of the laboring class to
come to this country. Every Chinese
traveler or student, business man o
professional man, should be given the
same right to enter to, and the same
courteous treatment In, this country
as are accorded to the student or
traveler, the business man or irO'
fesslonal men of any other nation.
Our laws and treaties should be so
framed as to guarantee to all China
men, save of the excepted coolie clas3,
the same right of entry to this conn
try and the same treatment while
here as la guaranteed to citizens of
any other nation. By executive ac
tion I am as rapidly as p6ssible putting
a stop to the1 abuses which have grown
up during many years In the ndminU
tratlon of this law. I can do a srood
deal, and will do a good deal oven
without the action of the congress
but I can nt do all tiint should be done
unless such action Is taken, !Ud that
action I mose earnestly hope will bs
taken. It is needed In our own !n-
terest and especially In the Inter est
of the Pacific slope an I f lit? noulh
Atlantic and Gulf states; for It is
short- sighted Indeed for us to permit
foreign competitors to drive us from
tho great markets of China. More
over, the action I ask Is demanded by
considerations that are higher than
mere Interest, for 1 ask It In the name
of what Is Just and right. America
should take the lead In establishing
international relations on the same
basis of honest and upright dealing
which we regard as essential as be

Tie Argonaut's
Prediction
Victor II. Metcalf, Secretary oi
the Department of Commerce and
Labor, is said to be likely to resign
from the Cabinet on account of the
new policy of the administration
regarding the Chinese exclusion
laws. Metcalf comes from Califor
nia, having rapidly risen in national affairs since his election to Con
gress not much over ten years ago, and it is evident that his wnole
political career is endangered by the rabid Pacific Coast sentiment
against the Chinese, if he docs anything calculated to "let down tin
bars." Yet it appears to be equally evident that down they must come,
if the new order, providing for more liberal treatment of those Chi
nese who have a right to enter the States, is a letting down, as it is said
on the coast to be. Californians declare that there will be wholesale
frauds under the new order. It is said to be worth a thousand dollars
to land a Chinese in America and five thousand dollars to land a
woman. Hence the openings for fraud arc evidently tremendous. The
temptations to official dishonesty among those allowed to issue certifi
cates in China will be very great.
To make matters worse for a Pacific Coast statesman who would
like at once to retain his standing at home and in Washington, it is
predicted that a few years will sec the whole exclusion policy abandon
ed altogether. The San Francisco Argonaut, which is certainly a far
seeing and able journal, predicts this development. In a very careful
and lengthy analysis of the situation from t'he national point of view,
it reaches the deliberate conclusion that events are marching rapidly
toward an abandonment of Chinese exclusion, and finally the Argo
naut announces its "deliberate conclusion" that "California is destined
to become a coolie state." Nothing that California can do can stop
this, says this most influential journal.
This is looking p far ahead of any policy yet announced. The Presi
dent has firmly declared that the exclusion policy is not to be aban
doned, and Secretary Taft while in Honolulu stated that "there is no
intention to let In any more coolies," applying the statement to Ha
waii as well as to the mainland. But history is being made rapidly in
the Orient. The Argonaut sees ahead an awakened China, able to
deal with other nations as Japan is, and it sees a growth of American
sentiment, away from the Pacific, in favor of allowing the Chinese to
enter. Hence the statement that nothing can save California from
becoming a coolie state.
If there is anything in such a prediction for California, what about
Hawaii? The wave of Asiatic immigration, if allowed to roll across Etc
the Facific, will strike our shores first. It is not going to start at so '
near a uaie tnat we nccu cuner 10 worry aooiu 11 or to oe pieascu ;u 1
the prospect according to our various wishes and opinions but the
prediction is not one to be cast lightly aside.

Within the World's Pair is a university which in'completeness surpasses
ike regular university in many ways, because there are assembled at the
great St. Louis Exposition many valuable and instructive features which no
university asquires. For this reason, university students, and even profes
sors, are taking advantage of the exceptional opportunity afforded. Each
exhibit of an educational character is demonstrated; thus the higher edu
cation is made to appeal to alt visitors. For instance, the Exposition does
not merely exhibit implements with placards showing that they are used for
life-saving, but it builds a lake and shows how the work is done. Currency
and coins are exhibited, but there arc also plants where money is made in
the sight of all. It is this method of conveying knowledge by means of ex
hibits that makes this great St. Louis World's Fair a university. If you
can not visit the Exposition in person, the next best thing is to secure our
magnificent scries of photographic reproductions which cover every impor
tant feature of the event, and which constitute a most potent educational
feature in every home.

is a standing joke with our friends up
north that "Canada gets $500 and the United Stntes gets the Chinaman!"
From the Mexican frontier the immigration of Chinese coolies goes on unin
terruptedly save for an occasional raid when the officials salve their can
sciences by delivering up a prisoner or two who obviously did not "deliver
up." Despite this, and with an utter disregard of the eternal fitness of
things, a cheap inspector in San Francisco deliberately insults a lady of
standing by making a pretence at deporting her. The exasperating part
of the application of the Federal exclusion law lies in the manner in which
it is handled by a few officials who do little more than sit on a wharf and
read over passenger lists. The exclusion law is intended for the exclusion
of coolie labor from the United States and the official who goes out of his
way to apply it further is deserving of severe censure if not of instant dis
missal. A peculiar fact in connection with the recent difficulties of some well
known Honolulu travelers at San Francisco is that the persons affected have
been going to and fro between here and San Francisco for twenty years or
more. They have landed over and
Union. Before annexation, when it would seem that examinations of Hono-,
lulu passengers must have been more careful if anything than it is now,
they freely entered San Francisco. What can have happened at that city
to cause this recent change? The conclusion seems irnsistible that official
zeal has been in some way reinforced by new motives. Incidentally the
recent indignities heaped upon passengers from here serve to show the utter
indecency and barbarism of -some of the provisions of the Chinese exclusion
law.

Decisive Battle

little else thnn mnsterlv stratecv in his movements. He was heavilv out-
numbered at Liaoyang, but he fought an engagement lasting several days, ,are obtalned b edification and filtra
infllcted very great loss on the enemy, and seems to be retreating safely to tlon- ln reffard t0 whlch llttle really

The great battle at Liaoyang has proved after all to be not the "decisive"
battle. There must be another, still farther north. Kuropatkin hag prob
ably planned his next stand and his next retreat and perhaps the game will
be continued to Mukden, even to Harbin. Then, if not before, the decisive
battle must come. In view of the size of Japan's population and the intense
war spirit, the Mikado will probably have no difficulty in meeting all the
reinforcements which can have reached Harbin by the time Kuropatkin
arrives, with a superior force. Probably Kuropatkin's repeated battles are
designed to delay the Japanese advance north, while every day is employed
in rushing reinforcements to Harbin. If this is the plan Kuropatkin is fairly
successful. But with her control of the sea assured Japan can send men
into Manchuria more quickly than Russia, and she has all the men neces
sary. They are willing to fight and die, and the loss of a few hundred thou
sand lives to their overpopulated country will not be felt. In fact it is re
ferred to by some critics as an actual benefit.
In the engagement just, fought it appears from first accounts that the
Russians somewhat vindicated their fighting reputation. They at first, re
pulsed Japanese at some points and captured some guns and two battalions,
and their final defeat seems to have been by no means a rout. Tokio up to
today claims little more than to have forced the retreat. There were no
large captures of prisoners and no important abandonment of Russian guns.
Possibly for the first time in the war, a force of Russians has withstood a
large general attack from Japanese armies and after inflicting almost blow
for blow, has retired to its next chosen position. At Port Arthur, the Rust
lian garrison holds on with grim determination, under conditions of terri
ble danger and hardship. All in all, Russia shows a slow improvement as
the war wears on. As has been said before, it is well that this is so. It is
better for the peace of the world that the contest should be more even than
it has been.

the manufacture of cocoanut oil. Says the consul: "The nut is broken by hand,
the meat is extracted and either dried in kilns, as in the Philippine Islands,
or in the sun, as in Cochin China. The oil is generally used for the manu
facture of soap, but of late years methods of refining It have been perfected,
with the result that a considerable proportion of the Alarseille oil is now
sold for alimentary purposes.
"The Inquiry as to the cost of working copra is difficult to answer satis

grades of vegetable oils as well. In a period of general stagnation, the best-
equipped manufacturers have made handsome fortunes in a single year.

LIGHT.
Is our lighting satisfactory? Many of us will say not. And the scienti
fic world agrees that the present light conditions are not satisfactory,
and that they may he bettered. A recent article upon the future of light
ing in the electrical' world has taken up this subject. This shows wiiat
other lights there are and there are pointed out what methods of lights
are needed. Our present lighting, no person who has to use it, can say
is satisfactory. Eyes were better in the days of wax candles than in the
days of incandescent lights. Would Lady Betty or Mistress Peggy
,liave ever looked sp alluring under the garish and steady lights of the
present, as they Jboked under the soft and shifting lights which added
to the charms of Kanelagh, of Vanahale, and of the countless ancestral
and somewhat ostentations homes of the old Dominion.
It is charming to think of those silks and brocades. Those priceless
laces which came over in very ordinary tubs of boats, those daintily
waved fans, those, beautifully clouded canes, those elegant ruffles, those
spotless, easy white shirts, which no laundress had ever desecrated with
her brazen starch. Fine women, fine men, fine horses, fine estates, fine
.weather, fine company, fine living. What a pity it could not last. What
the man who saw neither the beginning nor the end must have said was
"How lovely, how healthful, how truly idyllic, and yet how truly whole
some." Alas all that beauty began in misery and ended in downfall.
That was under' the old light. Now we have the new. We are be
ginning, but we can better it. We can make it more practical and more
wholesome to the eyes. Upon this there are divergent views, but there
can only be one .right path. This path an electric engineer is striving
or has been striving for. His views can be condensed into a few para
graphs: "Two things are .needed in electric lighting for the immediate future.
.One is a lamp of moderate power, moderate intrinsic brilliancy, good
color and high economy to replace or supplement the present glow
lamp. The other is a very efficient lamp of almost any power and bril
liancy, with plenty, of red and yellow in its spectrum, and capable of
operating when fully inclosed and practically without attention. The
first form of lamp' has its obvious uses laid out in advance. The second
would be almost equally valuable to the art, and could be utilized for in
direct lighting with the greatest advantage.
If the arcs with composite carbons could only be inclosed and run
without attention, they would fill the bill exactly. But no form of arc
yet devised can advantageously be employed as a concealed illuminant,
and, however efficient, it is easily beaten out by the glow lamp for inter
ior illumination. Almost any kind of illuminant will answer out of
doors, provided it does not require too much attention, but for the regu
lar work of interior lighting the incandescent lamp fully holds its own,
inefficient as it relatively is. Which of the suggested improved lights is
likely to appear first no one can tell ; perhaps both may come together.
The chances seem a little better for the first named than for a light of
high power; and the futile efforts which have thus far failed to obtain
either are evidence enough of1 the difficulty of the problem. " -
Lacking these improvements, the best hopes of electric lighting lie
in the skillful use of the illuminants that are now available, in a careful
adaptation of means to ends, and in a realization that illuminatio'jY'is not
a haphazard business where all the light that can be paid for is the'
proper amount to use. On-the contrary, light improperly employed is
not only useless, but harmful, and one light well placed is better than
two misplaced. If one can make ten common glow lamps do the work
of fifteen as now used, he has scored as great a success as if he had in
stalled a new form of twp-watt lamp. The final test of lighting is light

The car in which Abraham Lincoln
made the journey from Buffalo to Albany
on his way to Wahlingl',o for his firsl
inauguration is still running on the Au.
burn branch of the New York t'entral
railroad. The ceiling is decorated with
the national flag. and at one end is s
portrait of Lincoln and at the other onu
of Washington. The car. now known a
No. 540, was new in February. 1t61. and
was dcorated for the purlose of carry
ing the president. It is still in good con
dition and runs regularly.- Chicag
News.

GUGGENHEiM
FOR SENATE
DENVER. Dec. 15.—Simon Gug
genheim, of Jewish parentage,
member of one of the wealthiest
families in the country, recognized
head of the smelter trust, is likely
to be elected I'nited Statess sena
tor from Colorado to succeed Sena
tor T. M. Patterson. Politicians
say his election is assured. It was
predicted even before the last elec
tion, when Colorado elected a re
publican governor and legislature.
Guggenheim aided the republican
SIMON GUGGENHEIM,
This picture is from a snapshot.
He seldom wears an overcoat.
party in this campaign materially.
Guggenheim is modest. He has
as yet made no speech of conse
quence in this state, which it Is no
secret he desires to represent in
the national 'Millionaires' club."
He is rather a silent worker, sur
rounded by wise ones politically
who direct his political career. He
always figures as "among the hon
ored guests present" on state oc
casions, and, in a way, is popular.
He is a liberal contributor to char
itable and public movements. But
his political lieutenants, among
them the shrewdest of the republi
can party workers, make up for
their master's smiie-and-look-pleas
ant ways.
The residence of the smelter
trust magnate is "New York and
Denver."
It was common political gossip
in the last campaign that Guggen
heim "is to spend $1,000,000 to be
elected senator." At any rate, the
republicans conducted a costly
campaign.
Guggenheim as a youth was a
chemist. He spent some time in
the gold and silver districts of this
elate. With his father and broth
ers he organized the smelter trust.
Now all the precious metal mines
■pay tribute to this trtißt.
At this time Guggenheim is 39
years eld. He is a native of Phila-

ENFORCING
ORDINANCE
It is reported that City Boiler In
spector Vedder is now enforcing
the city ordinances in respect to
the firing of down town plants.
A number of blocks and plants
have ignored the ordinance for
years past and tbe inspector said
that it could not be enforced on
account of its being out of dale.
He had another ordinance drawn
up, but when he encountered tlie
Opposition of a number of local
business men who objected to any
laws governing power and heating
plants be abandoned the new law.
Being unable to secure a new ordi
nance it is now proposed to en
force the old.
CHILDREN BURN
BEFORE THEIR EYES
NORTH YAKIMA, Dec. IB. —
Three small children of Lawrence
Talbert burned to death yesterday
and another is fataly burned as
the result of a fire which destroyed
the residence. The father was in
the barn and the mother at the
pump when they noticed the
flames. The heat was so intense
they could not save the children,
who were sleeping upstairs.
LOSES LIBEL SUIT.
Pursuant to instructions yester
day afternoon from Judge Joiner,
the jury this morning found for
the defendant In the suit for $20,-
--000 damages brought against the
Spokesman-Review by M. H.
Whltehouse for alleged libel. The
plaintiff was unable to prove W.
H. Cowles publisher of tbe Spokes
man-Review in 1904, no list of
officers of the company being on
file.
DAUGHTER RAN AWAY.
Mrs. Swenson, of Genessee, Ida
ho, has notified the police to be
on the lookout for her daughter,
who ran away from home. The
girl Is under age, large, fair and
blue eyed.
Nat Levin, proprietor of the
Hub clothing store at Coeur
d'Aiene, Idaho, is in the city today
buying stock.

WHAT THE JAPS ARE REALLY DOING TO
CALIFORNIA!
Because of the intense excitement all over the country over
the "Japanese Question," The Press has arranged for a series
of articles that will show the true situation, which is alarming
enough from the standpoint of organized labor. Without douht,
the workingman has suffered by the coming of the little brown
men, who are giving the Pacific Coast states, particularly
California, a serious problem.
SAX FRANCISCO, Cal„ Dec. 15.
—The Japanese situation on the
Pacific coast is assuming alarming
proportions. The following inter
view with a purser on one of the
Pacific Mail steamers speaks for
itself:
"These Japs are wonderful peo
ple. Every manjack of them who'
is coming to this country is a
trained soldier, an apt scholar and
a hard worker. Aboard ship they
receive their training and go
through their drills on deck Japan
will soon have a standing army in
the United Stales of a half million
men, The idea of naturalization is
absurd. They don't want to be
come Americans, for they are here
in the interest of Japan, and would
gather beneath the banner of the
mikado in the event of war being
declared between the two coun
tries. They are positively danger
ous, a menace to the nation and an
enemy of organized labor."
The immigration from the Land
of the Rising Sun is looked upon
from the summit of the Rockies to
the Pacific ocean as a "brown
plague" that must be curtailed or
the social condition of the white
workingman will surely sink to the
level of the coolie.
The Idea of Japanese naturaliza
tion is as obnoxious to the west
erner as the emancipation of the
negro was to the most rabid south
erner on the eve of the civil war.
With one fell swoop President
Roosevelt almost took the wind out
of the sails of the Japanese Ex
clusion League, born in this city
not so many months ago, but which
has attained a maturity in this sec
tion of the country that thorough
ly voices the importance of the
Question.
A big fight is just beginning.
The Pacific coast states will
stand shoulder to shoulder In the
effort not only to prevent the fu
ture naturalization of tho Japanese,
but their ultimate exclusion from
this country on the same basis as
that existing against the Chinese.
In the east the people do not
seem to understand the situation.
They do not see the hordes of llt
browa fellows hard at work in
every class of labor while the white
workingman stands around idle.
They do not see the handler of
Japanese labor underbid the white
GRAND RAPIDS GOVERNED DV
INITIATIVE AND REFERENDUM
BY HERBERT S. BIGELOW.
Secretary Ohio State Referendum
League.
Grand Rapids, Mich., has shown
how a city, with initiative and
referendum, can govern itself with
out the aid or consent of politicians
and corporations.
Since 1905 the charter of Grand
Rapids has provided that 12 per
cent of the voters of the city may,
by petition, require the submission
of charter amendments to popular
vote. Also, by a 12 per cent peti
tion, they may require any fran
chise grant or other act of the city
council to be referred to a vote of
the people.
Last April the citizens' by initia
tive petition, brought three ques
tions to a direct vote.
The first provided for the right
to recall elective city officials by
special elections when petitioned
for by 25 per cent of the voters.
The second provided for the right
to recall appointive officers.
The third provided for the use of
the initiative on ordinances, as
well as upon charter amendments.
These amendments were all
carried by over three to one.
At the last November election
the people, by initiative petitions,
brought to a vote an amendment
which is well calculated to make a
junk heap of any political machine.
This Grand Rapids law deserves at
tention, for it means the end of the
power of the boss. It is a most im
portant step In the emancipation of
the cities. The Hon. Jesse Orton
of Grand Rapids, gives the follow
ing account of it:
"This is an amendment provid
ing for nonpartisan municipal elec
tions. Under It party primaries are
abolished and all city and ward of
ficers are nominated at a general
primary, at which ali electors may
vote. The names of candidates

FROM AN ORIENTAL LINER.
GOV.CHAMBERLAIN ON
JAP QUESTION
"The West is unalterably op
posed to joint occupancy of public
school rooms by Japanese or Chi
nese and our white children," said
Governor Chamberlain of Osegon
in an interview at Washington, D.
C. "We ore also against admission
of Chinese or Japanese coolies.
The present controversy over the
Japanese has assumed a phase
whlcti calls for application of more
common sense than has been mani
fested in some quarters. The in
terpretation of treaties rests sole
ly with the courts, and certainly
not with the executive branch of
the government, through the mili
tary, as President Roosevelt
threatens.
"It appears to me that the ad
ministration is inconsistent in at
tempting to force association In
the pubic schools of an inferior
race with white children, when
here in the District of Columbia
there is a system of separate
schools for whites and negroes.
"Let the president and congress
demonstrate beer the theory of
such association before trying to
force objectionable policies on the
people of the Pacific coast."
HE GETS THE LIMIT.
A Kurte was fined $100 and
appear on both the primary ballot
and the regular election ballot
without any party name or designa
tion, ami the voter must mark his
ballot separately for each office.
After the names of the two candi
dates receiving the highest number
of votes are placed on the ballot
as candidates for that office at the
ensuing election.
"The names of candidates are
placed on the primary ballot by
petition of not less than 100 and
not more than 300 voters, In case of
city offices, and by petition of not
less than 25 and not more than 75
voters in case of ward offices.
"This amendment was drafted by
four men who have been among
those most active in securing the
adoption of direct legislation prin
ciples. The draft was then sub
mitted to a larger gathering of the
friends of these principles perhaps
15 or 20 and after the making of
slight changes, the securing of sig
natures to the petition was com
menced.
"It was distinctly a flank move
ment on the old party machines. It
was received with favor by citizens
generally, without much reference
to party affiliations, and soon near
ly 8000 signatures were obtained,
only £100 being necessary. The
movement was placed in the hands
of an organization formed express
ly for the purpose, the Nonpartisan
Municipal Election league, and the
campaign was directly managed by
the executive committee of this or
ganization, consisting of four re
publicans, four democrats and one
prohibitionist, men well known for
party work and allegiance, but not
.identified with the poltical ma
chines of their respective parties.
"At the election Nov. 6. 190fi,
there was but little interest In the
state campaign, Michigan being
overwhelmingly republican. The
total vote on governor in the city
jof Grand Rapids was 12.944. The
total vote on the nonpartisan char
ter amendment was 12,215, H Sf>s
being for the amend men l and ;5,35u
securing money to bring more of
their kind to this country.
Tlie educated Japanese is an in
telligent fellow, and is welcomed to
society on a social basis with the
white man because of his learning.
The Japanese coolie, of which the
east sees but little, is a labor ma
chine that will in time, if allowed
to land promiscuously, displace the
American workman. They can
watch a carpenter build a house
and then do it themselves. They
underbid everybody in every line
of muscular effort.
And they are coming to this
country at the rate of 250,000 per
year. At this port of entry the
Pacific Mail Steamship company
alone brings in about 1000 a week.

DIES OF PNEUMONIA.
Henry Olson, who was brought
to Sacred Heart hospital suffering
with typhoid pneumonia, the re
sult of exposure in a railroad
camp, died yesterday. A brotljer
at Daisy, N. D., was notified. The
body is at the undertaking par
lors of Smith & Co.

his pockets were picked of a watch
at the Sloane minder trial.

the total vole. There was no ap
parent organised opposition to it,
but ot was quietly and vigorously
opposed by the machine forces of

These things have been accom
plished by the initiative. But Grand
Rapids has also made use of the
referendum on acts of the city
council. Last year the people, by
a mere threat to use the refer
endum, made tho council rescind a
franchise which it had granted to
the Standard Oil Co.
At the last November election
the people invoked the referendum
on an ordinance closing theaters on
Sunday. Last April the council, by
a vote of 1!) to 5 had ordered the
theaters closed on Sunday. By a
petition of 12 per cent of the voters
those in favor of open theaters won
by a small majority of (!14 out of a
total vote of 13,176. It is worthy
of note that this vote was 232 in
excess of the vote cast for govern
or at the same election.
With the initiative and referen
dum each city is bound to become,
like Grand Rapids, a political ex
perimental station: and with the
cities free to solve their own prob
lems in their own way, in accord
ance with the will of the majority
of the voters, progress will be ac
celerated.
The people may make mistakes.
But with the Initiative and referen
dum they can correct them as soon
as they learn better. Their govern
ment will then be exactly what they
wish, and will improve as they im
' prove. The freedom to experiment
|ls the condition of progress.
